steps.label: What problem are we solving?
steps.help: Frame the pain or gap — customer-visible, not solution-shaped.

steps.label: Who are the target users or beneficiaries?
steps.help: Segments, roles, or contexts — enough that discovery research can aim.

steps.label: What is the core value proposition?
steps.help: Why this matters now — outcome-oriented, one sharp thesis.

steps.label: How will we know this succeeded?
steps.help: Observable signals, adoption, or business metrics — not a feature list.

steps.label: What constraints must we respect?
steps.help: Time, compliance, platform, dependencies, or non-negotiables.

steps.label: What is explicitly in scope?
steps.help: Bullet-style lines — each line becomes a scope item.

steps.label: What is explicitly out of scope?
steps.help: Prevents silent creep — name the tempting adjacent work we are not doing.

steps.label: What assumptions are we making?
steps.help: Each line is one assumption to validate in discovery.

steps.label: Open questions for discovery (drafts)
steps.help: Each line becomes a draft open question on the card for the discovery column.

steps.label: Understanding summary
steps.help: Synthesise what you heard — this mirrors Phase 3 of `/pdlc-brief-custom`.
